questions.question: Who counts as a "stakeholder"?
options: Anyone who affects or is affected by the work
Only the people who fund the project
Only the end users of the product
Only managers above the team
questions.explanation: A stakeholder is anyone who affects or is affected by the work — funders, consumers, blockers, dependents, and governors alike, not just sponsors or users.

questions.question: On the Power/Interest grid, how should you treat a high-power, high-interest stakeholder?
options: Manage Closely — partner, co-decide, no surprises
Monitor — minimal effort
Keep Informed — demos only
Ignore until they complain
questions.explanation: High power + high interest = Manage Closely (partner and co-decide). High/low = Keep Satisfied; low/high = Keep Informed; low/low = Monitor.

questions.question: What is the cardinal sin of stakeholder management?
options: Surprising a high-power stakeholder
Sending too many status updates
Inviting stakeholders to the Sprint Review
Using a Power/Interest grid
questions.explanation: Surprising a high-power stakeholder is the cardinal sin — it destroys trust. The "no surprises" principle (early bad news with options) is how you keep their confidence.

questions.question: What is the "constructive no" when a stakeholder demands work that breaks the Sprint Goal?
options: "Yes, and here's the trade-off" — make the fixed-capacity cost explicit and let the PO decide
A flat "no" to protect the team
Silently absorb the work to keep the peace
Escalate to your manager before responding
questions.explanation: Never a flat no — say "yes, and here's the trade-off": capacity is fixed, so name what drops or moves, and hand the prioritization decision to the Product Owner who owns priority. That makes you a partner, not a gatekeeper.

questions.question: A Scrum Master has formal authority to direct stakeholders.
questions.explanation: The Scrum Master has no authority over stakeholders — only influence. Credibility (not a title) earns followership; the moment you try to order people, you've run out of influence.

questions.question: How should you report progress to senior leadership?
options: In outcomes, flow metrics, and the decision needed — lead with the headline and the ask
As a detailed task list of everything the team did
Primarily with the team's velocity number
Only at the end of the quarter, in a long deck
questions.explanation: Executives read top-down and are time-poor: lead with the headline outcome and the ask, support with a couple of flow metrics (lead time, cycle time, throughput) and trends, and surface risk early with options — not a task list.

questions.question: Why should you avoid reporting velocity to executives?
options: It's a team-internal planning number they misread as productivity, and it gets gamed once it becomes a target
Velocity is too hard to calculate accurately
Executives are not allowed to see team metrics
Velocity always overstates progress
questions.explanation: Velocity is for the team's own forecasting. Executives misread it as a productivity score, and turning it into a target invites gaming (Goodhart's Law). Report outcomes and flow instead.

questions.question: A VP keeps pulling engineers off the Sprint mid-flight. What's the best approach?
options: Privately understand the need, make the cost visible with data, reframe it as a prioritization decision for the PO, and offer a sustainable channel
Refuse the VP outright to protect the Sprint Goal
Let the VP take whoever they want, since they outrank you
Quietly extend the Sprint to absorb the disruption
questions.explanation: Understand the real need with curiosity, quantify the cost ("cycle time jumped 6→11 days"), and reframe it as a PO prioritization decision (the PO owns the trade-off, not the grabbed developer). Offer a sustainable channel (Planning, or reserved ad-hoc capacity); escalate the pattern, not the person, if it persists.

questions.question: What does "protect the team with a permeable membrane, not a wall" mean?
options: Let the right things in (real feedback, PO-routed priority changes) and deflect the wrong things (drive-by pings, silent scope creep)
Block all stakeholder contact with the team
Forward every stakeholder request directly to developers
Only allow contact during the Daily Scrum
questions.explanation: A permeable membrane admits real customer feedback and genuine PO-routed priority changes while deflecting random pings and scope creep. An informed stakeholder interrupts less than an anxious one, so good protection increases the team's contact with reality.

questions.question: In a RACI matrix, what is the rule about the "Accountable" designation per row?
options: Exactly one Accountable per row — otherwise nobody truly owns the decision
At least two Accountable people for redundancy
Accountable and Responsible must always be the same person
Accountable means "told after the decision"
questions.explanation: RACI = Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed. There must be exactly one Accountable per row (or nobody owns it). Consulted = input before; Informed = told after — never blur them. RACI is for cross-boundary decisions, not micro-assigning tasks inside a self-managing team.

questions.question: Which of the following are among Cialdini's classic six principles of influence? (Select all that apply.)
options: Reciprocity
Social Proof
Scarcity
Coercion
questions.explanation: Cialdini's six are Reciprocity, Commitment/Consistency, Social Proof, Authority, Liking, and Scarcity (a seventh, Unity, was added later). Coercion is not one of them — these are ethical influence levers, not force.

questions.question: Surfacing bad news to leadership early, before they have to ask, is a trust deposit rather than a liability.
questions.explanation: Early bad news is uncomfortable but builds trust — it gives leaders time to act and proves they can rely on your signal. A leader surprised by a slip was failed by their reporting line.

questions.question: In the Trust Equation, which factor sits in the denominator (i.e. more of it *reduces* trust)?
options: Self-orientation
Credibility
Reliability
Intimacy
questions.explanation: Trust = (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) ÷ Self-orientation. Visible self-interest is the denominator — the more you appear to serve yourself rather than the outcome, the lower the trust.

questions.question: A high-power stakeholder feels left out and is now undermining the work. What is the root cause and best recovery?
options: Information starvation — go directly, acknowledge the gap, listen for the real interest, and bring them inside with a tuned communication channel
They are simply irrational; ignore them until they calm down
Win the argument publicly to reassert your position
Escalate to HR to formally reprimand them
questions.explanation: This is a Keep-Satisfied stakeholder who spiked into Manage-Closely because they were surprised (a mapping miss). The root cause is almost always information starvation; the cure is a tuned channel (standing brief, Sprint Review invite, early sight of decisions), not winning an argument.

questions.question: A stakeholder's position on the Power/Interest grid is fixed and only needs to be mapped once.
questions.explanation: Positions shift — a Keep-Satisfied VP whose pet feature is suddenly at risk spikes into Manage-Closely overnight. Re-plot the grid every release rather than treating it as a one-time exercise.

questions.question: What does the term "HiPPO" refer to in decision-making, and how do you counter it?
options: The Highest-Paid Person's Opinion — counter it by answering with data
A high-priority production outage — counter it with an expedite lane
A heavyweight integration process — counter it by automating
A high-performing product owner — counter it with delegation
questions.explanation: HiPPO = the Highest-Paid Person's Opinion driving decisions by authority rather than evidence. The antidote is to bring data (cycle time, conversion, defect trends) so decisions rest on signal, not seniority.

questions.question: The stakeholder Salience model classifies stakeholders along which three dimensions?
options: Power, legitimacy, urgency
Power, interest, attitude
Reach, impact, confidence
Cost, value, risk
questions.explanation: The Salience model (Mitchell, Agle & Wood) uses power, legitimacy, and urgency — a richer classification than the two-axis Power/Interest grid for deciding whose claims demand attention.

questions.question: What makes an escalation to leadership effective rather than just complaining?
options: A specific ask and a by-when, plus options and a recommendation
A detailed list of everyone who is to blame
A request to simply 'make the problem go away'
Waiting until the deadline so the urgency is obvious
questions.explanation: Effective escalation pairs the impediment with a specific ask, a by-when, options, and a recommendation — giving leaders a decision to make, not just a problem to absorb. Surfacing it early (not at the deadline) is part of the discipline.
